Damaging and mission selling is taught in the book, so I won't spend much time on it, but if you want to make a note, page 37 through 40 in the Alderman Sales Library, a sales water book, you can do that. 40 teaches a technique of damaging and mission selling in it, an extremely effective copy text. Yeah, the idea is that the truth of the matter is nobody's got a perfect product and anybody with any brains knows it. So everything has its great benefits and everything has at least one weakness. You gain credibility in people's lives by admitting your weakness and telling them what that is and why it shouldn't bother them. Rather than just ignoring the issue, you know, by immediately admitting that you have this one thing that's not so great, everything else you're claiming is now more believable, and this guy is clearly willing to tell us the truth. If you want to make the argument that you could go to the AARP list and mail the Physician's Visual Insurance package without Dennis James and the Physician's Visual Insurance package with Dennis James, and one's going to pull 3.2%, the other one's going to pull 2%, but there's almost no crossover because the one with Dennis feels different people than the one with Dennis. And then really what you should do is you should be mailing both to the same universe because you make money even at 2%. That's a viable argument. If the same argument applies to two different ads in the same magazine, I could never get US Gold to do it. We tested it. US Gold was going to do it in print ads. And he was running his big display ad with pictures. I wrote a copy-intensive ad. No pictures. All copy. The full-page copy ad running in the same magazine at the same time as the full-color ad. The full-color ad would pull like two times ad cost. The copy ad would pull one and a half times ad cost. So he tested it and said, screw it, we're staying with the color ad. My argument is what he should have been doing is running both of them forevermore because the one and a half percent and the 2% both paid money and there was almost no duplication.
